be, that is the doubt
That racks this fragile vessel of my soul.
Must I endure the heavy, grinding wheel
Of outrageous fortune, or with steel in hand,
Bid a defiance to this sea of grief?
To die, to sleep—and in that sleep, perchance,
To dream of things this wretched earth denies.
Ay, there’s the rub that stays the eager blade,
For in that silent, everlasting dark,
What terrors wake when mortal coils are shed?
We bear the tyrant’s lash, the proud man’s contumely,
The pangs of love despised, the law’s delay,
When with a bare bodkin we might

returned_text:
, Romeo! wherefore art thou Romeo?
Deny thy father and refuse thy name;
Or, if thou wilt not, be but sworn my love,
And I'll no longer be a Capulet.
'Tis but thy name that is my enemy;
Thou art thyself, though not a Montague.
What's Montague? it is nor hand, nor foot,
Nor arm, nor face, nor any other part
Belonging to a man. O, be some other name!
What's in a name? that which we call a rose
By any other name would smell as sweet;
So Romeo would, were he not Romeo call'd,
Ret

returned_text:
of our discontent
Made glorious summer by this sun of York;
And all the clouds that lour'd upon our house
In the deep bosom of the ocean buried.
Now are our brows bound with victorious wreaths;
Our bruised arms hung up for monuments;
Our stern alarums changed to merry meetings,
Our dreadful marches to delightful measures.
Grim-visaged war hath smooth'd his wrinkled front;
And now, instead of mounting barbed steeds
To fright the souls of fearful adversaries,
He capers nimbly in a lady's chamber
To the lascivious pleasing of a lute.
But I, that am not shaped for sportive tricks,
Nor made to court

returned_text:
, lend me your ears!
I come to weep for Rome, whose golden crown
Is tarnished by the breath of civil strife.
The Tiber flows with tears of widowed wives,
And noble blood runs cold in marble halls
Where once we sat in peaceful governance.
Look on these wounds, this torn and bleeding state,
Where brother turns his blade on brother’s breast,
And tell me, is this greatness? Is this Rome?
We chased the shadows of ambition's ghost
And found a tyrant's shroud. O, heavy day!
The gods themselves do veil their eyes in shame
To see the ruin of our glorious race.
Let not your hearts be hardened to my

returned_text:
to a winter’s storm?
Thou art more fierce, more ruinous and cold.
Rough winds do shake the temples of my form,
And all my youth is turned to barren mold.
Sometime too hot the eye of heaven shines,
Yet thy pale gaze doth freeze my bleeding heart;
And every fair from fair my soul declines,
By thy sharp cruelty and treacherous art.
But thy eternal winter shall not fade,
Nor lose possession of the grief thou owest,
Nor shall Death brag thou wand'rest in his shade,
When in these weeping lines to hell thou goest.
So long as men can breathe, or eyes can see,
So long lives this,
